title: Runs batted in

infobox.value: or **runs batted in** (**RBI**[[1]](#cite_note-1)) is a [statistic in baseball](/wiki/Baseball_statistics "Baseball statistics") and [softball](/wiki/Softball "Softball") that credits a [batter](/wiki/Batting_(baseball) "Batting (baseball)") for making a play that allows a [run](/wiki/Run_(baseball) "Run (baseball)") to be scored (except in certain situations such as when an [error](/wiki/Error_(baseball) "Error (baseball)") is made on the play). For example, if the batter bats a [base hit](/wiki/Hit_(baseball) "Hit (baseball)") which allows a teammate on a higher base to reach [home](/wiki/Baseball_field#Home_base "Baseball field") and so score a run, then the batter gets credited with an RBI.

["RBI"](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/RBI). *Merriam-Webster.com Dictionary*. Merriam-Webster. [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20200930054544/https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/RBI) from the original on September 30, 2020. Retrieved October 30, 2020.

[The Accurate RBI Record of Babe Ruth](http://sabr.org/research/accurate-rbi-record-babe-ruth) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20160927051519/http://sabr.org/research/accurate-rbi-record-babe-ruth) September 27, 2016, at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine"). *SABR Website*. Retrieved on September 14, 2016.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Runs batted in](/w/index.php?title=Runs_batted_in&redirect=no "Runs batted in"))

Statistic used in baseball and softball

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Albert_Pujols_DSC_5191.jpg/250px-Albert_Pujols_DSC_5191.jpg)](/wiki/File:Albert_Pujols_DSC_5191.jpg)

St. Louis Cardinals' Albert Pujols, shown here in 2008, passed Babe Ruth's 2,214 official RBI in October 2022. Pujols finished his Major League Baseball career with 2,218 RBI and second place on the [all-time list](/wiki/List_of_Major_League_Baseball_career_runs_batted_in_leaders "List of Major League Baseball career runs batted in leaders").

A **run batted in** or **runs batted in** (**RBI**[[1]](#cite_note-1)) is a [statistic in baseball](/wiki/Baseball_statistics "Baseball statistics") and [softball](/wiki/Softball "Softball") that credits a [batter](/wiki/Batting_(baseball) "Batting (baseball)") for making a play that allows a [run](/wiki/Run_(baseball) "Run (baseball)") to be scored (except in certain situations such as when an [error](/wiki/Error_(baseball) "Error (baseball)") is made on the play). For example, if the batter bats a [base hit](/wiki/Hit_(baseball) "Hit (baseball)") which allows a teammate on a higher base to reach [home](/wiki/Baseball_field#Home_base "Baseball field") and so score a run, then the batter gets credited with an RBI.

Before the [1920 Major League Baseball season](/wiki/1920_Major_League_Baseball_season "1920 Major League Baseball season"), runs batted in were not an official [baseball statistic](/wiki/Baseball_statistics "Baseball statistics"). Nevertheless, the RBI statistic was tabulated—unofficially—from 1907 through 1919 by baseball writer [Ernie Lanigan](/wiki/Ernie_Lanigan "Ernie Lanigan"), according to the [Society for American Baseball Research](/wiki/Society_for_American_Baseball_Research "Society for American Baseball Research").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Common nicknames for an RBI include "ribby" (or "ribbie"), "rib", and "ribeye". The plural of "RBI" is a matter of "(very) minor controversy" for baseball fans:[[3]](#cite_note-plural_controversy-3) it is usually "RBIs", in accordance with the usual practice for pluralizing [initialisms](/wiki/Initialism "Initialism") in English;[[4]](#cite_note-sometimes_rbi_plural-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) however, some sources use "RBI" as the plural, on the basis that it can stand for "runs batted in".[[3]](#cite_note-plural_controversy-3)[[4]](#cite_note-sometimes_rbi_plural-4)

## Major League Baseball rules

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Run_batted_in&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Major League Baseball rules")]

The 2018 edition of the Official Baseball Rules of [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") (MLB), **Rule 9.04 Runs Batted In**, reads:[[6]](#cite_note-6)

> A run batted in is a statistic credited to a batter whose action at bat causes one or more runs to score, as outlined in Rule 9.04.
>
> (a) The official scorer shall credit the batter with a run batted in for every run that scores
>
> :   (1) unaided by an error and as part of a play begun by the batter's safe hit (including the batter's home run), sacrifice bunt, sacrifice fly, infield out, or fielder's choice unless Rule 9.04(b) applies;
> :   (2) by reason of the batter becoming a runner with the bases full (because of a [base on balls](/wiki/Base_on_balls "Base on balls"), an award of first base for being [touched by a pitched ball](/wiki/Hit_by_pitch "Hit by pitch") or for interference or obstruction); or
> :   (3) when, before two are out, an error is made on a play on which a runner from third base ordinarily would score.
>
> (b) The official scorer shall not credit a run batted in
>
> :   (1) when the batter grounds into a force [double play](/wiki/Double_play "Double play") or a [reverse-force double play](/wiki/Double_play#Scoring_of_double_plays "Double play"); or
> :   (2) when a fielder is charged with an [error](/wiki/Error_(baseball) "Error (baseball)") because the fielder muffs a throw at first base that would have completed a force double play.
>
> (c) The official scorer's judgment must determine whether a run batted in shall be credited for a run that scores when a fielder holds the ball or throws to a wrong base. Ordinarily, if the runner keeps going, the official scorer should credit a run batted in; if the runner stops and takes off again when the runner notices the misplay, the official scorer should credit the run as scored on a fielder's choice.

From 1980 to 1988, the [game-winning RBI](/wiki/Game-winning_RBI "Game-winning RBI") was an additional statistic used in MLB.

## Criticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Run_batted_in&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Criticism")]

The perceived significance of the RBI is displayed by the fact that it is one of the three categories that compose the [triple crown](/wiki/Triple_crown_(baseball) "Triple crown (baseball)"). In addition, career RBIs are often cited in debates over who should be elected to the [Hall of Fame](/wiki/National_Baseball_Hall_of_Fame_and_Museum "National Baseball Hall of Fame and Museum"). However, critics, particularly within the field of [sabermetrics](/wiki/Sabermetrics "Sabermetrics"), argue that RBIs measure the quality of the lineup more than it does the player himself. This is because an RBI can only be credited to a player if one or more batters preceding him in the batting order have reached base (the exception to this being a [home run](/wiki/Home_run "Home run"), in which the batter is credited with driving himself in, not just those already on base).[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-moneyball-8) This implies that better offensive teams—and therefore, the teams in which the most players get on base—tend to produce hitters with higher RBI totals than equivalent hitters on lesser-hitting teams.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## RBI leaders in Major League Baseball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Run_batted_in&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: RBI leaders in Major League Baseball")]

### Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Run_batted_in&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Career")]

Further information: [List of Major League Baseball career runs batted in leaders](/wiki/List_of_Major_League_Baseball_career_runs_batted_in_leaders "List of Major League Baseball career runs batted in leaders")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Hank_Aaron_1960.png/250px-Hank_Aaron_1960.png)](/wiki/File:Hank_Aaron_1960.png)

Hank Aaron, All-time career leader in RBI with 2,297.

Totals are current through 2025 (regular season). Active player is in **bold**.

1. [Hank Aaron](/wiki/Hank_Aaron "Hank Aaron") – 2,297
2. [Albert Pujols](/wiki/Albert_Pujols "Albert Pujols") - 2,218
3. [Babe Ruth](/wiki/Babe_Ruth "Babe Ruth") – 2,214
4. [Alex Rodríguez](/wiki/Alex_Rodr%C3%ADguez "Alex Rodríguez") – 2,086
5. [Cap Anson](/wiki/Cap_Anson "Cap Anson") - 2,075
6. [Barry Bonds](/wiki/Barry_Bonds "Barry Bonds") – 1,996
7. [Lou Gehrig](/wiki/Lou_Gehrig "Lou Gehrig") – 1,995
8. [Stan Musial](/wiki/Stan_Musial "Stan Musial") – 1,951
9. [Ty Cobb](/wiki/Ty_Cobb "Ty Cobb") – 1,944
10. [Jimmie Foxx](/wiki/Jimmie_Foxx "Jimmie Foxx") – 1,922
11. [Eddie Murray](/wiki/Eddie_Murray "Eddie Murray") – 1,917
12. [Willie Mays](/wiki/Willie_Mays "Willie Mays") - 1,909

### Season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Run_batted_in&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Season")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Hank_Greenberg_1937_cropped.jpg/250px-Hank_Greenberg_1937_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hank_Greenberg_1937_cropped.jpg)

Hank Greenberg, Hall of Famer and 2-time MVP

1. [Hack Wilson](/wiki/Hack_Wilson "Hack Wilson") (1930) – 191
2. [Lou Gehrig](/wiki/Lou_Gehrig "Lou Gehrig") (1931) – 185
3. [Hank Greenberg](/wiki/Hank_Greenberg "Hank Greenberg") (1937) – 183
4. [Jimmie Foxx](/wiki/Jimmie_Foxx "Jimmie Foxx") (1938) – 175
5. [Lou Gehrig](/wiki/Lou_Gehrig "Lou Gehrig") (1927, 1930) – 173

### Game

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Run_batted_in&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Game")]

Further information: [List of Major League Baseball single-game runs batted in leaders](/wiki/List_of_Major_League_Baseball_single-game_runs_batted_in_leaders "List of Major League Baseball single-game runs batted in leaders")

* 12 RBIs[[10]](#cite_note-TenOrMoreRbiInOneGame-10)
  + [Jim Bottomley](/wiki/Jim_Bottomley "Jim Bottomley") (September 16, 1924)
  + [Mark Whiten](/wiki/Mark_Whiten "Mark Whiten") (September 7, 1993)
* 11 RBIs[[10]](#cite_note-TenOrMoreRbiInOneGame-10)
  + [Wilbert Robinson](/wiki/Wilbert_Robinson "Wilbert Robinson") (June 10, 1892)
  + [Tony Lazzeri](/wiki/Tony_Lazzeri "Tony Lazzeri") (May 24, 1936)
  + [Phil Weintraub](/wiki/Phil_Weintraub "Phil Weintraub") (April 30, 1944)
* 10 RBIs[[10]](#cite_note-TenOrMoreRbiInOneGame-10)
  + By 12 MLB players, most recently **[Shohei Ohtani](/wiki/Shohei_Ohtani "Shohei Ohtani")** on September 19, 2024

### Inning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Run_batted_in&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Inning")]

Further information: [List of Major League Baseball single-inning runs batted in leaders](/wiki/List_of_Major_League_Baseball_single-inning_runs_batted_in_leaders "List of Major League Baseball single-inning runs batted in leaders")

1. [Fernando Tatís](/wiki/Fernando_Tat%C3%ADs "Fernando Tatís") (April 23, 1999) – 8
2. [Ed Cartwright](/wiki/Ed_Cartwright "Ed Cartwright") (September 23, 1890) – 7
3. [Alex Rodriguez](/wiki/Alex_Rodriguez "Alex Rodriguez") (October 4, 2009) – 7

### Postseason (single season)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Run_batted_in&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Postseason (single season)")]

1. **[Adolis García](/wiki/Adolis_Garc%C3%ADa "Adolis García")** (2023) – 22[[11]](#cite_note-AdolisBreaks-11)
2. [David Freese](/wiki/David_Freese "David Freese") (2011) – 21[[12]](#cite_note-DavidFreeseBreaks-12)
3. **[Corey Seager](/wiki/Corey_Seager "Corey Seager")** (2020) – 20[[11]](#cite_note-AdolisBreaks-11)
4. [Scott Spiezio](/wiki/Scott_Spiezio "Scott Spiezio") (2002) – 19[[12]](#cite_note-DavidFreeseBreaks-12)
5. [Sandy Alomar Jr.](/wiki/Sandy_Alomar_Jr. "Sandy Alomar Jr.") (1997) – 19[[12]](#cite_note-DavidFreeseBreaks-12)
6. [David Ortiz](/wiki/David_Ortiz "David Ortiz") (2004) – 19[[12]](#cite_note-DavidFreeseBreaks-12)
